name: Atlantis Antarctica
description: The Atlantis Antarctica theory posits that the mythical island of Atlantis was not located in the Atlantic Ocean, but rather in Antarctica. Proponents suggest that Antarctica was once a temperate landmass, home to an advanced civilization that was subsequently frozen over during a cataclysmic event, possibly a pole shift or a global flood. This civilization, they claim, was Atlantis. The theory often draws on pseudoscientific interpretations of ancient maps, geological anomalies, and supposed evidence of artificial structures beneath the ice. It is often linked to conspiracy theories about suppressed knowledge and hidden agendas of governments or secret societies. Mainstream science overwhelmingly rejects this theory. Geological evidence indicates that Antarctica has been located at the South Pole for millions of years, far predating the supposed existence of Atlantis. The ice sheet is also far too thick to allow for the preservation of large-scale structures. Furthermore, the theory relies on selective interpretations of data and ignores established scientific principles. The notion of a sudden pole shift capable of causing such rapid glaciation lacks credible scientific support. While the idea of a lost civilization resonates with some, the Atlantis Antarctica theory lacks any compelling evidence and contradicts established scientific understanding of Antarctic geology and climate history.
subtitle: Lost Civilization Frozen in Ice?

claims.claim: Antarctica was once a temperate landmass capable of supporting a civilization.
claims.evidence: Ancient maps like the Piri Reis map showing Antarctica without ice, and alleged geological anomalies.
claims.criticism: The Piri Reis map's depiction of Antarctica is likely a misinterpretation of known coastlines, and geological anomalies are often natural formations.

claims.claim: Advanced structures and artifacts from Atlantis are buried beneath the Antarctic ice.
claims.evidence: Unexplained satellite imagery and claims of radar anomalies suggesting artificial structures.
claims.criticism: Satellite imagery and radar anomalies are often misinterpreted or explained by natural geological features and ice formations. No confirmed artifacts have been found.

claims.claim: A cataclysmic event, such as a pole shift, caused the rapid glaciation of Atlantis.
claims.evidence: Ancient myths and legends describing global floods and catastrophic events.
claims.criticism: While pole shifts have occurred over geological timescales, the idea of a sudden, civilization-destroying pole shift lacks scientific evidence. Ancient myths are subject to interpretation and cannot be taken as literal historical accounts.

keyFigures.name: Charles Hapgood
keyFigures.role: Proposed the Earth Crust Displacement theory, often cited by proponents.

keyFigures.name: Graham Hancock
keyFigures.role: Popularized the theory in his books, linking it to ancient mysteries.

keyFigures.name: David Childress
keyFigures.role: Explored alleged evidence for ancient civilizations in Antarctica.

keyFigures.name: Dr. Michael Salla
keyFigures.role: Promotes the idea that Antarctica contains hidden alien technology and information about ancient civilizations.

keyFigures.name: Dr. Kenneth Feder
keyFigures.role: Archaeologist and skeptic who has debunked many claims of ancient civilizations in Antarctica.

timeline.event: Charles Hapgood publishes 'Earth's Shifting Crust', influencing later theories.
The Piri Reis map gains popularity as supposed evidence of pre-glacial Antarctica.
Graham Hancock and other authors popularize the Atlantis Antarctica theory in their books.
The theory continues to circulate online and in alternative media.

sources.author: Feder, Kenneth L.
sources.work: Frauds, Myths, and Mysteries: Science and Pseudoscience in Archaeology
sources.title: Feder, Kenneth L.

sources.author: Flem-Ath, Rand & Rose
sources.work: When the Sky Fell: In Search of Atlantis
sources.title: Flem-Ath, Rand & Rose
